insights: Evanskevin submitted 100 identical expense claims (amount: 51285, description: 'Water risk class understand PM do big occur.') with identical opened_at and processed_date timestamps, confirming a system-level data duplication error—not repeated user submissions.

After deduplication, the baseline dataset contains 499 unique claims across 50 users, with zero exact duplicate groups on user+amount+short_description—indicating no genuine repeated identical claims remain.

No near-duplicate expense claims exist in the deduplicated dataset (using 5% amount tolerance and ≥50% description similarity), and all candidate pairs have identical or near-identical timestamps—further ruling out behavioral repetition due to fraud or policy misunderstanding.
Zero users exhibit low amount variation (coefficient of variation < 0.2) across 3+ claims, eliminating patterns consistent with habitual or template-based erroneous resubmission.

All 100 of evanskevin’s duplicate claims are in 'Processed' state with no time gaps or status transitions, contradicting fraud indicators (e.g., repeated submissions after rejection) or policy confusion signals (e.g., resubmission following decline or correction requests).

The dataset spans 10 months (2024-01-02 to 2024-10-29) and includes 598 raw claims, yet contains no evidence of time-separated, behaviorally motivated repeated expense claims by any user—only one isolated data integrity incident.
summary: The analysis conclusively finds no evidence of fraudulent or policy-driven repeated expense claims in the dataset; the only repetition observed is a single system-level data duplication event involving 100 identical claims by evanskevin, all sharing identical timestamps and processing states. Post-deduplication, the 499-claim baseline shows zero exact or near-duplicate claim patterns across users, amounts, and descriptions—and no users display low-variation spending behavior suggestive of habitual resubmission. This confirms the dataset reflects high operational integrity in claim submission behavior, with the sole anomaly attributable to a data ingestion or synchronization failure rather than user misconduct or policy ambiguity. Next focus should be root cause investigation of the evanskevin timestamp duplication in upstream systems, not behavioral monitoring or policy training interventions.
